text: The claim that the reffing was biased on England vs Ghana makes no sense
label: Hot Take

text: Now, don't get me wrong, Ghana deserved a penalty clear as day; however, anybody trying to claim that the ref was biased towards England needs their head checked. Yes, that should have been called, but Ghana also managed to foul England 24 times while receiving just one card (you can get cards for repeated rules violations). If the ref had cut that shit out by giving cards out (which he should've), England would have a much higher chance of scoring. Also, England losing wouldn't be bad for business; in fact, it would be the opposite for FIFA because they'd still probably go through, and upsets create hype and money, so FIFA would have no real motivation to rig it for England.
label: Analysis

text:
The way the media talk about Harry Kane versus Cristiano Ronaldo says a lot about media bias. For starters, let me start off by saying that both Kane and Ronaldo are fantastic strikers. And several of the very best to do it in recent times. The point of my post is not to downplay Kane at the expense of propping up Ronaldo. Or vice versa. Rather, my point is far too many media members are not actually properly analyzing the two players remotely objectively or in good faith.

And again, that is literally impossible in this format. Seriously. If you lose two games 1-0 and win one 7-0 to end up on 3 pts level with someone else, you must have beaten a team that the other side lost to. There’s no other way to get there. Even more than that, you must have beaten a side with more points to get there.

If you have 6 pts and lost to another team with 6 pts, guess what, you must have beaten someone they lost to in order to end up level with them.

GD means that how you do in all the games matters, HTH means you can discard the other results and just care about one.

text: Allowing third-place qualifiers trades stakes for narrative. Instead of watching top teams walk a tightrope just to survive the group stage, we are taking solace in watching mediocre teams grind out draws that, in the long run, will mean little in terms of  the shape the tournament ultimately takes. This isn't a criticism of Cinderella performances. World Cup has always provided a stage for audacious upsets. It's a criticism of a format that seems to prioritize these one-offs, to the point that it not only compromises the quality of matches at large, but undermines the significance of the upse
label: Hot Take

text:
All that's changed is at what point teams are allowed to play "safe."

The new head-to-head tiebreaker demands that ALL teams not play "safe" to *start* the tournament.  They can't enter any game planning to grind out a draw or maintain a narrow loss.

The new setup doesn't reward cautious planning.  It rewards wins and can put you at jeopardy of being grouped if you can't secure any points in your first two matches.

The new setup rewards the stronger starts and/or more in-form teams of the tournament with favorable positioning for the next round.  The teams that hit the ground running and wo
label: Hot Take

text:
He just made a self-pass, he is still in possession of the ball, the defender can't just block and trip another player without contesting the ball first. 

In football data collection and refereeing, a player does not need to be physically touching the ball every second to maintain possession. Pushing the ball into space to run past a defender is an active, controlled football action, not a loss of control.

For any referee, because the striker is still in possession, a tackle that takes out the player without touching the ball is penalized as a foul.

Do you even understand the rules?
label: Analysis

World Cup is no longer the pinnacle of the game. UCL is.

The most notable trend in modern football is coordinated pressing. Even forwards are expected to contribute, and it's not just aimlessly closing down the opponent with the ball.

text:
I get the Norway argument, but I still think Japan are being underrated here.  
Injuries matter, of course, but Japan’s biggest strength is not just individual quality. It’s the structure, pressing, discipline and the fact that they usually know exactly how they want to play. That can survive injuries better than a team that depends heavily on two elite players.  
Norway clearly have the higher ceiling because of Haaland and Ødegaard, but I’m still not convinced they are the better tournament team. If a top side manages to isolate those two, do Norway have enough control and depth to adapt?
label: Analysis

label: Analysis

text: Soldiers do actually overheat and suffer from heat exhaustion constantly but they wear heavy gear because surviving shrapnel is more important than athletic agility. Football is entirely different. Adding rigid protection to a shoe isn't a weight or heat issue, it completely ruins ball control. Players need a soft, paper thin surface to feel, grip and curve the ball accurately. Plus armor introduces risk compensation. If players know their feet are protected they will stamp and slide into tackles with even more reckless force, leading to far worse knee and ankle injuries for everyone.
label: Analysis
